Since communication is not defined as an atomic proposition, and prop_0 is conditional, we model the required activities as holding globally during start-up, and start-up ends (no longer in start-up) once these hold. Without explicit start-up mode and completion activities as propositions, we can specify that until the end, prop_1 and prop_3 (and possibly prop_0) must hold. This means globally (G) prop_1 and prop_3 (and prop_0 if present): G(prop_1 & prop_3 & prop_0). If prop_0 is not present, we use only prop_1 and prop_3: G(prop_1 & prop_3).

The sentence describes that in start-up mode, prop_0 (if it exists), prop_1, and prop_2, as well as prop_3, and finally prop_4 after some required activities, should hold. Since LTL does not specify start-up or completion of required activities unless they are atomic propositions, and we are not given such, this translates to the requirement that eventually (in start-up mode) prop_0 (optional), prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, and then prop_4 after some activities happen. For the translation, we assume that eventually all these propositions will be true, with a possible order implied: prop_4 after the others. explanation: The statement says that in start-up mode, ‘prop_0’ (if needed—it may not exist), ‘prop_1’, ‘prop_2’, and ‘prop_3’ must occur, and ‘prop_4’ can only occur after these have completed (i.e., not before them). In LTL, to require all props (except prop_4) to happen at some point, we use conjunctions of eventualities: F prop_0 & F prop_1 & F prop_2 & F prop_3. To express that prop_4 can only occur after these, we say: (¬prop_4) U (prop_0 & prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3). This means prop_4 is not allowed until all others have occurred; after that, it may happen any time. If prop_0 is optional, it can be omitted, but if we must include it, it will appear as written.
